annotations.label: Shor's algorithm

anchor.snippet: Shor's algorithm is a quantum algorithm for finding the prime factors of an integer

annotations.note: Mathlib has no model of quantum algorithms or quantum computation.

annotations.label: Polynomial-time factoring on a quantum computer

anchor.snippet: Shor's algorithm runs in polynomial time

annotations.note: No asymptotic-complexity or quantum-runtime analysis exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integer factorization is in BQP

anchor.snippet: the integer factorization problem is in complexity class BQP

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define complexity classes such as BQP.

annotations.label: Asymptotically faster than the number field sieve

anchor.snippet: asymptotically faster than the most scalable classical factoring algorithm

annotations.note: No classical factoring complexity (number field sieve) is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Breaking RSA via factoring

anchor.snippet: RSA can be broken if factoring large integers is computationally feasible

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the RSA cryptosystem or its security reduction.

annotations.label: No classical polynomial factoring algorithm known

anchor.snippet: no classical algorithm is known that can factor integers in polynomial time

annotations.note: This is an open empirical claim about complexity, not a Mathlib-formalizable theorem.

annotations.label: Polynomial-complexity factoring circuit

anchor.snippet: factoring integers can be done with a polynomial complexity circuit on an ideal quantum computer

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of quantum circuits or circuit complexity.

annotations.note: A physical experiment, outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Continued-fraction recovery theorem
annotations.note: Legendre's theorem (`Real.exists_rat_eq_convergent`): if |ξ−p/q|<1/(2q²) then p/q is a convergent, which is the recovery guarantee used here.
